prompt: As of June 2026, what is the state of the art for PRIVACY-PRESERVING fine-tuning/distillation and confidential inference on customer data? Cover: GPU TEE / confidential computing (H100/H200 CC, Azure/GCP confidential GPUs), differential privacy for fine-tuning, PII redaction pipelines for training data, and 'your data never leaves' architectures (without using that exact phrase). Give what is practical in 2026 (overhead, availability) and cite vendor docs/papers with dates. Then: for a platform that proxies a customer's API traffic and distills a model from it, what is the strongest credible privacy posture to offer in 2026 (redaction + tenant fencing + optional confidential GPU), and what must be honestly disclaimed about data handling?
content:
**As of June 2026, GPU confidential computing (NVIDIA Hopper H100/H200 TEEs) is generally available on major clouds with low-to-moderate overhead for inference and fine-tuning workloads, particularly when using optimizations like CUDA graphs.**[[1]](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azureconfidentialcomputingblog/general-availability-azure-confidential-vms-with-nvidia-h100-tensor-core-gpus/4242644)[[2]](https://www.computer.org/csdl/proceedings-article/iiswc/2025/491700a084/2bNw7sv0Qx2)

NVIDIA introduced hardware-rooted TEEs on H100 (first widely available GPU TEE), with general availability around May 2025; H200 support followed and is production-ready in multiple deployments. Key features include protected GPU memory, execution, registers, and PCIe traffic encryption, anchored by an on-die root of trust. It supports multi-GPU setups (e.g., 8x H100/H200).[[3]](https://forums.developer.nvidia.com/t/h200-confidential-compute-fully-supported/325755)[[4]](https://phala.com/learn/How-Nvidia-Enable-Confidential-AI)

- **Azure**: Confidential VMs with NVIDIA H100 GPUs reached general availability in September 2024 (NCCadsH100v5-series, AMD SEV-SNP + H100; regions include East US2 and West Europe). Overhead is described as negligible for most models, especially larger ones or with CUDA graphs enabled; smaller models see higher relative impact from PCIe encryption and kernel overhead.[[1]](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azureconfidentialcomputingblog/general-availability-azure-confidential-vms-with-nvidia-h100-tensor-core-gpus/4242644)[[5]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/confidential-computing/gpu-options)
- **GCP**: Confidential VMs with NVIDIA H100 GPUs on A3-series accelerator-optimized machines moved from preview (as of late 2024) toward broader availability, supporting data-in-use protection for AI/ML workloads.[[6]](https://docs.cloud.google.com/confidential-computing/confidential-vm/docs/create-a-confidential-vm-instance-with-gpu)[[7]](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/expanding-confidential-computing-for-ai-workloads-next24)

**Overhead summary (inference-focused, applicable to fine-tuning/distillation loops)**: Studies report 4–8% throughput penalties on H100 CC that diminish with larger batch sizes and models; some deployments claim <5% overall (95–99% of native performance). Specific scenarios involving frequent model loading/swapping can see higher impacts (e.g., 20–30% latency increase, 45–70% throughput reduction in one 2025 evaluation), but these are mitigable. Fine-tuning overhead follows similar patterns when using parameter-efficient methods. Attestation and multi-tenant isolation are supported.[[2]](https://www.computer.org/csdl/proceedings-article/iiswc/2025/491700a084/2bNw7sv0Qx2)[[8]](https://phala.com/learn/Confidential-LLMs)

**Differential privacy (DP) for fine-tuning** has matured into a practical tool, especially combined with parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA. Foundational work (e.g., 2022 ICLR paper on DP fine-tuning of large LMs) demonstrated strong privacy-utility trade-offs using DP-SGD with parameter-efficient updates. Subsequent research (2025 papers) explores user-level DP, impacts across methods/privacy budgets, and defenses against extraction/membership inference attacks, providing practical guidance on epsilon selection and methodology. It is feasible for domain-specific adaptation without full retraining, though utility degrades at very tight privacy budgets; it is often layered with other controls.[[9]](https://openreview.net/forum?id=Q42f0dfjECO)[[10]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.21036v2)[[11]](https://research.google/blog/fine-tuning-llms-with-user-level-differential-privacy/)

**PII redaction pipelines** are production-grade and commonly deployed pre-training or fine-tuning. Approaches include rule-based + ML/LLM detectors in gateway or application layers, self-hosted pipelines (e.g., for chat/RAG/agents), and domain-specific tuning (HIPAA/GDPR). Best practice is to benchmark precision/recall on representative customer traffic, as performance varies by dataset, language, and entity type; hybrid systems (redact obvious structured PII, audit high-confidence cases) are recommended. These run efficiently as a preprocessing step before any model training or inference.[[12]](https://philterd.ai/blog/)[[13]](https://www.truefoundry.com/blog/pii-redaction-llm-gateway-vs-application)

**Architectures that keep customer data local** (or minimize exposure) include on-premises or customer-controlled GPU deployments for fine-tuning/distillation, federated or device-cloud collaborative fine-tuning, and proxy/gateway layers that perform redaction or synthetic data generation before any upstream processing. RAG-style retrieval keeps raw data in the customer environment while only sending processed queries. Confidential computing augments these by protecting data-in-use even when processing occurs in a provider cloud.[[14]](https://www.spheron.network/blog/llm-inference-on-premise-vs-cloud/)

**For a platform proxying customer API traffic to distill a model**, the strongest credible 2026 posture combines:
- **Mandatory PII redaction** at the proxy/gateway layer (customer-vetted or self-hosted pipeline, benchmarked on their traffic).
- **Strong tenant fencing/isolation** (per-customer environments, separate key management, no cross-tenant data mixing, attested isolation).
- **Optional confidential GPU execution** for the distillation/fine-tuning workload itself (H100/H200 CC on Azure/GCP or equivalent, with attestation).

This stack (redaction + fencing + optional TEE) provides hardware-rooted protection during compute, cryptographic isolation between tenants, and preprocessing to remove obvious identifiers. It is deployable today with existing vendor offerings and aligns with regulated workloads.

**Honest disclaimers** that must be included:
- Redaction is imperfect; it can miss context-dependent, obfuscated, or novel PII (false negatives are inevitable without perfect semantic understanding).
- Distilled models can still encode statistical patterns or indirect signals from the (redacted) data, creating residual privacy or IP leakage risks via model inversion, membership inference, or extraction attacks—even under DP.
- The proxy necessarily observes (redacted) traffic metadata and content during processing; full end-to-end encryption with customer-held keys is possible but adds complexity.
- TEEs protect against certain host/cloud provider attacks but do not eliminate all side-channel risks, implementation bugs, or supply-chain issues; attestation verifies the TEE but not higher-level application logic.
- No technique provides absolute guarantees against all future attacks or regulatory interpretations; customers retain responsibility for data classification, consent, and downstream model governance.
- Distillation inherently involves using customer interaction data to improve a (potentially shared or derived) model unless explicitly scoped to customer-only outputs.

This posture is among the strongest practically achievable in 2026 without fully on-premises customer-controlled infrastructure.
